basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: The Poets (al-Shuʿarāʾ) opens on the Prophet's pain, almost consumed with grief that his people will not believe — and God consoles him: “Your only charge is to convey.” Then unfolds the great procession of prophets, each account sealed by the twofold refrain: “Truly in that is a sign, yet most of them are not believers — and indeed your Lord is the Mighty, the Merciful.” Moses confronts Pharaoh, and the magicians, first come for a wage, fall down prostrate and believe in the Lord of the worlds, preferring truth to every threat. Abraham disowns the idols and celebrates his Master, “He who created me and guides me, who feeds me, heals me, will cause me to die then bring me to life,” before praying for the Day “when neither wealth nor sons avail, save one who comes to God with a sound heart (qalb salīm)” — that heart of which Qushayrī says it is the one delivered from the will of its own soul and from all familiarity with other than God. Then come Noah, Hūd, Ṣāliḥ, Lot and Shuʿayb, each one denied, each one aided while his people perish. Then the trustworthy Spirit (al-rūḥ al-amīn) brings the Revelation down upon the Prophet's heart, in a clear Arabic tongue; it is not the devils who bear it, they who descend upon every sinful liar. The sūra closes on the poets whom the erring follow, wandering from valley to valley and saying what they do not do — save those who believe, do good, and remember God without ceasing. Qushayrī thus makes of the whole movement an ascent toward the sound heart, the sole provision of the Day when every support collapses.
themes: the Prophet's grief and the consolation
the refrain of the sign and the Mighty-Merciful
Moses, Pharaoh and the converted magicians
Abraham and the sound heart (qalb salīm)
the procession of the denied prophets
the trustworthy Spirit and the Revelation upon the heart

versets.trad: Ṭā. Sīn. Mīm.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gathers the readings of the Ancients on these isolated letters: the Ṭā signals the purity of His glory and the holiness of His elevation, the Sīn the radiance of His omnipotence, the Mīm the majesty of His grandeur in eternity. By another key, the Ṭā points to the tree Ṭūbā, the Sīn to the Lote-tree of the Limit, and the Mīm to the name of Muḥammad who, on the Night of Ascension, rose beyond every creature, settling in nothing but his Lord.

mots.def: three opening letters, object of symbolic readings.

versets.trad: These are the verses of the clear Book.
versets.commentaire: These are the proofs of Our generosity, the marks of Our favor, the witnesses of Our kindness, says Qushayrī, by which God shows His friends the truth of His promise. The Book is “clear” (mubīn) for it expounds and heals: clarity and remedy, light and radiance, glad tidings and proof for those whom We have confirmed in faith.

mots.racine: ك ت ب / ب ي ن

versets.ar: لَعَلَّكَ بَٰخِعٌ نَّفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: laʿallaka bākhiʿun nafsaka allā yakūnū muʾminīna
versets.trad: Perhaps you will consume yourself with grief because they do not believe.
versets.commentaire: Here Qushayrī touches the Messenger's passion: through your burning desire that they believe and your dread of their refusal, you are near to slaying your own soul out of grief that they forsake faith. Then comes consolation: no blame lies upon you, O Muḥammad, for Our decree is unchangeable; your sole duty is to convey.

mots.racine: ب خ ع / ن ف س
mots.def: bakhaʿa: to consume, exhaust with grief.

versets.ar: إِن نَّشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ءَايَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْنَٰقُهُمْ لَهَا خَٰضِعِينَ
versets.tr: in nashaʾ nunazzil ʿalayhim mina s-samāʾi āyatan fa-ẓallat aʿnāquhum lahā khāḍiʿīna
versets.trad: If We willed, We could send down upon them a sign from heaven, before which their necks would bow [in humiliation].
versets.commentaire: God announces His power to obtain what He wills of His servants: He could compel them to believe against their will. Qushayrī notes that to refrain from imposing faith is no weakness, for incapacity to attain one's purpose would stain the divinity with a defect. He thus leaves free the one He wills to test.

mots.racine: ع ن ق / خ ض ع

versets.ar: وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن ذِكْرٍ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَٰنِ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā yaʾtīhim min dhikrin mina r-raḥmāni muḥdathin illā kānū ʿanhu muʿriḍīna
versets.trad: No fresh reminder comes to them from the All-Merciful but that they turn away from it.
versets.commentaire: Every new Law We institute, every messenger We send, they turn from without even pondering the proof, says Qushayrī. Were they to examine the signs of the messengers with care, their truthfulness would become clear to them; but the portion of abandonment assigned to them in the prior decree prevents them from believing.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / ح د ث

versets.ar: فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا۟ فَسَيَأْتِيهِمْ أَنۢبَٰٓؤُا۟ مَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ
versets.tr: fa-qad kadhdhabū fa-sa-yaʾtīhim anbāʾu mā kānū bihi yastahziʾūna
versets.trad: They have cried lies; but soon will come to them the tidings of that which they mocked.
versets.commentaire: They have denied and persisted in their denial, says Qushayrī: so the outcome of their deeds will come to them as a severe chastisement, and they will taste the burden of their associationism. What is laughed at today returns tomorrow as crushing news.

versets.ar: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَمْ أَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ
versets.tr: awa-lam yaraw ilā l-arḍi kam anbatnā fīhā min kulli zawjin karīmin
versets.trad: Have they not looked upon the earth, how many a noble kind We have caused to grow therein?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī marvels at the innumerable: the species that spring up in spring escape all counting; each has its color, taste, scent, form, flower, leaf — until expression fails and allusion grows subtle. In all of it are signs for whoever looks and reflects.

mots.racine: ز و ج / ك ر م

versets.ar: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: inna fī dhālika la-āyatan wa-mā kāna aktharuhum muʾminīna
versets.trad: Truly in that is a sign, yet most of them are not believers.
versets.commentaire: First passage of the refrain that will give the sūra its rhythm. Qushayrī recalls that signs are offered to whoever enjoys inner sight (man istabṣara); the sign endures, but the closed heart does not receive it. The blindness is not in the sign — it is in the gaze.

versets.trad: And indeed your Lord is the Mighty, the Merciful.
versets.commentaire: The second refrain, the seal of every account. Qushayrī unfolds the two Names: the Mighty (al-ʿAzīz), the Subduer whom none subdues, the Powerful whom none constrains, the Inaccessible whom none compels; the Merciful (al-Raḥīm), the Benefactor of His servants, who wills the felicity of His friends.

versets.ar: وَإِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبُّكَ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنِ ٱئْتِ ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّٰلِمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-idh nādā rabbuka mūsā ani ʾti l-qawma ẓ-ẓālimīna
versets.trad: And when your Lord called Moses: “Go to the wrongdoing people,
versets.commentaire: God calls Moses to confront Pharaoh. Qushayrī observes that Moses, knowing the adversary violent and blinded by pride, began to raise objections — not in refusal or rebellion, but seeking grace and dispensation — until he understood that the command was firm and the decree irrevocable.

mots.racine: ق و م / ظ ل م

versets.trad: the people of Pharaoh. Will they not fear?”
versets.commentaire: “Will they not fear?”: the call to taqwā opens the mission. Qushayrī ties this sending to the firmness of the divine decree: the command to Moses was decisive, the judgment upon him sealed, and the fear of God is the first step every messenger demands.

mots.def: ittaqā: to fear God, guard oneself.

versets.ar: قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ
versets.tr: qāla rabbi innī akhāfu an yukadhdhibūni
versets.trad: He said: “My Lord! I fear they will call me a liar,
versets.commentaire: Moses lays bare his human frailty. Qushayrī notes that he asked to be aided by Aaron and to make him share in the message — not from cowardice but from clear sight of the task's harshness. The truthful servant does not mask his fear before his Lord.

versets.ar: وَيَضِيقُ صَدْرِى وَلَا يَنطَلِقُ لِسَانِى فَأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَٰرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-yaḍīqu ṣadrī wa-lā yanṭaliqu lisānī fa-arsil ilā hārūna
versets.trad: and my breast tightens and my tongue is not free. So send for Aaron!
versets.commentaire: The breast that tightens and the knotted tongue: Moses confesses the messenger's inner ordeal. Qushayrī recalls that he asked to associate Aaron in the prophethood; the weight of the Real dilates or constricts, and it is again God who loosens the tongue of whom He chooses.

mots.racine: ض ي ق / ص د ر
ط ل ق / ل س ن

versets.ar: وَلَهُمْ عَلَىَّ ذَنۢبٌ فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ
versets.tr: wa-lahum ʿalayya dhanbun fa-akhāfu an yaqtulūni
versets.trad: They have a charge against me, and I fear they will kill me.”
versets.commentaire: Moses recalls the man he had slain and the blood-debt Pharaoh claimed, says Qushayrī: “I fear they will kill me.” But the servant who fears for his life remains under the gaze of the One who will answer: Nay! Go with Our signs: I am with you.

versets.ar: قَالَ كَلَّا فَٱذْهَبَا بِـَٔايَٰتِنَآ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّسْتَمِعُونَ
versets.tr: qāla kallā fa-dhhabā bi-āyātinā innā maʿakum mustamiʿūna
versets.trad: He said: “Nay! Go, both of you, with Our signs: We are with you, listening.
versets.commentaire: “Kallā” is a word of repulsion and awakening: it shall not be as you fear; turn away from this apprehension, renders Qushayrī. Then the sovereign promise: I am with you both through aid, strength, sufficiency and mercy; the upper hand and authority shall be yours; I hear what you say and what is said to you.

mots.def: kallā: by no means; repulsion and awakening.

versets.ar: فَأْتِيَا فِرْعَوْنَ فَقُولَآ إِنَّا رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
versets.tr: fa-ʾtiyā firʿawna fa-qūlā innā rasūlu rabbi l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: Go, both of you, to Pharaoh and say: ‘We are the messengers of the Lord of the worlds,
versets.commentaire: According to a report Qushayrī cites, Moses and Aaron besieged Pharaoh's door a whole year without finding access; then they presented the message, and he met them with denial. The Lord of the worlds sends, and the tyrant refuses to hear.

mots.def: rasūl: messenger (singular for two, marking the message's unity).

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ل م
mots.def: rabb al-ʿālamīn: Master of all creatures.

versets.trad: that you send forth with us the Children of Israel.’ ”
versets.commentaire: The demand is plain: free the Children of Israel. Qushayrī sets this request within the long patience of the two messengers at the tyrant's door: the mission is to deliver the oppressed and restore to God His enslaved servants.

mots.def: banū Isrāʾīl: the people of Jacob.

versets.ar: قَالَ أَلَمْ نُرَبِّكَ فِينَا وَلِيدًا وَلَبِثْتَ فِينَا مِنْ عُمُرِكَ سِنِينَ
versets.tr: qāla a-lam nurabbika fīnā walīdan wa-labithta fīnā min ʿumurika sinīna
versets.trad: He said: “Did we not raise you among us as a child, and you stayed with us many years of your life?
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh recalls Moses's childhood at his court to humble him. Qushayrī will answer later that if the nurturing of creatures creates a right, the nurturing of God is far worthier of the servant's reverence: the debt to the Creator outweighs every debt to men.

versets.ar: وَفَعَلْتَ فَعْلَتَكَ ٱلَّتِى فَعَلْتَ وَأَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَٰفِرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-faʿalta faʿlataka llatī faʿalta wa-anta mina l-kāfirīna
versets.trad: Yet you committed the deed you committed, and you are among the ungrateful.”
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh calls Moses “ungrateful” (kāfir, here in the sense of denying a favor). Qushayrī shows the tyrant measuring all by the favor received and the blood-debt, unable to conceive that a divine command might make obligatory what man judges a fault.

mots.def: kāfir: one who denies a favor.

versets.ar: قَالَ فَعَلْتُهَآ إِذًا وَأَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ
versets.tr: qāla faʿaltuhā idhan wa-anā mina ḍ-ḍāllīna
versets.trad: Moses said: “I did it then, when I was among the erring.
versets.commentaire: Moses finds no answer but admission and acknowledgment, says Qushayrī: “Yes, all that was.” The candor of the truthful servant does not dodge the past; yet God then honored him with prophethood. The admission is here the threshold of election.

versets.ar: فَفَرَرْتُ مِنكُمْ لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ فَوَهَبَ لِى رَبِّى حُكْمًا وَجَعَلَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ
versets.tr: fa-farartu minkum lammā khiftukum fa-wahaba lī rabbī ḥukman wa-jaʿalanī mina l-mursalīna
versets.trad: So I fled from you when I feared you. Then my Lord granted me wisdom and made me one of the messengers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī accepts the literal sense: Moses feared for his life and fled, and flight, for lack of strength, is blameworthy in no one's eyes. Another reading: he fled for fear a divine punishment might fall upon them through their associationism. Then God granted him wisdom and the mission.

versets.ar: وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَىَّ أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ
versets.tr: wa-tilka niʿmatun tamunnuhā ʿalayya an ʿabbadta banī isrāʾīla
versets.trad: Is that a favor you cast in my face, that you enslaved the Children of Israel?”
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh counted among his favors that he had let Moses live among the Israelite children he was slaughtering. Moses turns the argument back, per Qushayrī: is that a favor you reproach me with? Would your enslavement of Israel count as a favor? That is neither grace nor kindness on your part.

versets.trad: Pharaoh said: “And what is the Lord of the worlds?”
versets.commentaire: The accursed one asked out of ignorance, says Qushayrī, and he used “mā” (what), the tool by which one inquires about what lacks reason. To ask “what is” the Lord is already to lower Him to the rank of things: the question betrays the blindness of the one who poses it.

mots.def: mā: interrogative used for the non-rational.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ل م

versets.ar: قَالَ رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ
versets.tr: qāla rabbu s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-mā baynahumā in kuntum mūqinīna
versets.trad: Moses said: “The Lord of the heavens and the earth and all that is between them — if you would have certainty.”
versets.commentaire: Moses turns away from the word ‘what’ and what it implies, and answers with what befits the description of the Most High: Lord of the heavens, the earth, and all between. To the “what” that reifies, he opposes the Name that designates universal lordship, offered to certainty (yaqīn).

versets.trad: He said to those around him: “Do you not hear?”
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh, astonished at the answer, calls his entourage to witness. Qushayrī notes that Moses answered: Your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers, without being drawn off course; the tyrant, however, then swerves from the straightness of speech and slides into insult.

versets.trad: He said: “Your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers of old.”
versets.commentaire: Moses extends the lordship to past generations: He who governs you governed your fathers too. Qushayrī observes that before this upright word Pharaoh swerved from the path of sensible discourse and took to folly, charging Moses with madness. Truth so disturbs pride that it makes it curse.

versets.ar: قَالَ إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ
versets.tr: qāla inna rasūlakumu lladhī ursila ilaykum la-majnūnun
versets.trad: Pharaoh said: “Your messenger who was sent to you is surely possessed.”
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh calls Moses mad because he affirms the existence of a god other than himself, says Qushayrī; yet nothing in Moses's conduct was madness. The prophet does not stoop to answer folly with folly: he continues praising his Lord.

versets.ar: قَالَ رَبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ
versets.tr: qāla rabbu l-mashriqi wa-l-maghribi wa-mā baynahumā in kuntum taʿqilūna
versets.trad: Moses said: “The Lord of the East and the West and all that is between them — if you would understand.”
versets.commentaire: To the insult Moses replies with a new widening: Lord of the East, the West and all between. Qushayrī glosses: if you are among those who have reason and discernment, you will recognize this Master of the horizons. Sound reason leads to the one lordship.

versets.ar: قَالَ لَئِنِ ٱتَّخَذْتَ إِلَٰهًا غَيْرِى لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمَسْجُونِينَ
versets.tr: qāla la-ini ttakhadhta ilāhan ghayrī la-ajʿalannaka mina l-masjūnīna
versets.trad: Pharaoh said: “If you take a god other than me, I shall surely make you one of the imprisoned.”
versets.commentaire: The tyrant turns to threats: “I shall do, I shall act... if you take a god other than me,” Qushayrī reports. Pride that thinks itself a god has nothing left but prison and force to answer proof. The threat is the confession that the argument has failed.

versets.trad: Moses said: “Even if I bring you something manifest?”
versets.commentaire: Moses meets the threat with the promise of a manifest proof. Qushayrī, following the narrative, shows Moses calm before the fury: the manifest thing (shayʾ mubīn) will soon confound the tyrant who will hide, trembling, beneath his throne.

mots.racine: ش ي أ / ب ي ن

versets.ar: قَالَ فَأْتِ بِهِۦٓ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّٰدِقِينَ
versets.tr: qāla fa-ʾti bihi in kunta mina ṣ-ṣādiqīna
versets.trad: He said: “Bring it then, if you are of the truthful!”
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh dares Moses to produce his sign. The tyrant's challenge will recoil upon him: Qushayrī reports that when the staff became a serpent, Pharaoh leapt up and hid beneath his couch, shuddering with fear, unmasked in his pretension.

versets.ar: فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ ثُعْبَانٌ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: fa-alqā ʿaṣāhu fa-idhā hiya thuʿbānun mubīnun
versets.trad: So he cast his staff, and behold, it became a manifest serpent.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī recounts the wonder: he cast his staff, and God turned it into a serpent that nearly swallowed Pharaoh's dwelling with all within it. The miracle, for Qushayrī, is the turning-over of essences (qalb al-aʿyān), a sign proper to prophethood.

mots.racine: ث ع ب / ب ي ن

versets.ar: وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُۥ فَإِذَا هِىَ بَيْضَآءُ لِلنَّٰظِرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-nazaʿa yadahu fa-idhā hiya bayḍāʾu li-n-nāẓirīna
versets.trad: And he drew forth his hand, and behold, it was white to the beholders.
versets.commentaire: The second sign: the hand that shines. With the staff-serpent, these two proofs should have sufficed; but the hardened heart takes refuge in denial. Qushayrī shows Pharaoh unmasked, yet resorting to magic after the evidence, “like the man who relapses into his sickness.”

versets.ar: قَالَ لِلْمَلَإِ حَوْلَهُۥٓ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَسَٰحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ
versets.tr: qāla li-l-malaʾi ḥawlahu inna hādhā la-sāḥirun ʿalīmun
versets.trad: He said to the chiefs around him: “This is indeed a learned sorcerer;
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh gathers his council and, says Qushayrī, they all agreed that he had bewitched them; after the sign, he returned to his blindness. To call the miracle “learned magic” is the evasion of one who refuses to bow before the evidence.

mots.racine: س ح ر / ع ل م

versets.ar: يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُم بِسِحْرِهِۦ فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ
versets.tr: yurīdu an yukhrijakum min arḍikum bi-siḥrihi fa-mādhā taʾmurūna
versets.trad: he wishes to drive you from your land by his magic; so what do you command?”
versets.commentaire: The tyrant stirs the fear of losing land and power. Qushayrī shows the proud one, shaken, turning to his council: “what do you decide?” He who claimed divinity begs the counsel of men — proof that his power was mere appearance.

versets.ar: قَالُوٓا۟ أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ وَٱبْعَثْ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَٰشِرِينَ
versets.tr: qālū arjih wa-akhāhu wa-bʿath fī l-madāʾini ḥāshirīna
versets.trad: They said: “Put him and his brother off a while, and send into the cities gatherers
versets.commentaire: The council proposes to delay and to gather the magicians. Qushayrī, following the thread, here prepares the scene where human cunning will dare to challenge the divine sign — while the decree has already settled that the upper hand will belong to Moses and Aaron.

mots.def: ḥāshir: one who musters the crowd.

versets.trad: to bring you every learned sorcerer.”
versets.commentaire: The most expert sorcerers are summoned to face Moses. Qushayrī will observe that these men, come for a wage, will end prostrate: he who works for another for a reward is not like one whose work is for God — and yet grace will seize them.

versets.trad: So the magicians were gathered at the appointed time of a known day,
versets.commentaire: The day is fixed, the magicians assembled. Qushayrī sees in this gathering the stage where God will turn the cunning over: Moses's staff will swallow all they have brought, and from these experts come to conquer will issue the first believers.

versets.trad: and it was said to the people: “Will you assemble,
versets.commentaire: The crowd is summoned to the spectacle. Qushayrī, in this narrative, shows the multitude coming to bet on the magicians; but the decree has already placed victory on the side of truth. Crowds follow appearance; the Real disposes of the outcome.

versets.ar: لَعَلَّنَا نَتَّبِعُ ٱلسَّحَرَةَ إِن كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْغَٰلِبِينَ
versets.tr: laʿallanā nattabiʿu s-saḥarata in kānū humu l-ghālibīna
versets.trad: that we may follow the magicians if they are the victors?”
versets.commentaire: The crowd wants to follow whoever wins. Qushayrī contrasts this allegiance to gain with the attachment of God's friends, who follow not apparent victory but truth. To follow “those who prevail” is the religion of interests, not of hearts.

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا جَآءَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ قَالُوا۟ لِفِرْعَوْنَ أَئِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا إِن كُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْغَٰلِبِينَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā jāʾa s-saḥaratu qālū li-firʿawna a-inna lanā la-ajran in kunnā naḥnu l-ghālibīna
versets.trad: When the magicians came, they said to Pharaoh: “Will there be a reward for us if we are the victors?”
versets.commentaire: The magicians demand a wage. Qushayrī pierces the baseness of their aim: they proclaimed the meanness of their ambition, and he guaranteed them their pay. Yet he who works for another for a reward is not like one whose work is for God. And yet God was about to transmute these mercenaries into witnesses.

versets.ar: قَالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ
versets.tr: qāla naʿam wa-innakum idhan la-mina l-muqarrabīna
versets.trad: He said: “Yes, and you will then be among those brought near.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes this verse a lesson on false nearness: whoever seeks the favor of a creature, the humiliation he reaps from it surpasses the honor he hoped for. But those brought near to God (al-muqarrabūn) are the first to enter into His presence on the Day of meeting; the rest remain prisoners of the veil.

versets.ar: قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰٓ أَلْقُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُم مُّلْقُونَ
versets.tr: qāla lahum mūsā alqū mā antum mulqūna
versets.trad: Moses said to them: “Cast what you are to cast.”
versets.commentaire: Moses, assured of his Lord, invites the magicians to cast first. Qushayrī notes this serenity of the sustained servant: he lets the cunning unfold so as the better to let it vanish. Trust in God has no need to strike the first blow.

versets.ar: فَأَلْقَوْا۟ حِبَالَهُمْ وَعِصِيَّهُمْ وَقَالُوا۟ بِعِزَّةِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ ٱلْغَٰلِبُونَ
versets.tr: fa-alqaw ḥibālahum wa-ʿiṣiyyahum wa-qālū bi-ʿizzati firʿawna innā la-naḥnu l-ghālibūna
versets.trad: So they cast their ropes and staffs, saying: “By the might of Pharaoh, we shall surely be the victors!”
versets.commentaire: They swear “by the might of Pharaoh”: their strength hangs only on a mortal support. Qushayrī had said it: he who has no helper but a promise of payment and the giving of a bribe will soon be abandoned. To swear by the creature is to build on sand.

versets.ar: فَأَلْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ
versets.tr: fa-alqā mūsā ʿaṣāhu fa-idhā hiya talqafu mā yaʾfikūna
versets.trad: Then Moses cast his staff, and behold, it devoured what they had falsified.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī describes the decisive instant: Moses's staff swallowed all they had brought, then became a staff again, and the very essences of their ropes vanished. Truth does not fight the false: it absorbs it and reduces it to nothing.

versets.trad: Then the magicians were cast down prostrate,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī underlines the irresistible character of this collapse into the light: the magicians were cast down prostrate — as if seized despite themselves by the evidence of the Real. Those who knew magic best were the first to recognize what surpassed it.

versets.trad: saying: “We believe in the Lord of the worlds,
versets.commentaire: From mercenaries they become believers. Qushayrī makes their turning the proof that what counts is the endings (al-ʿibra bi-l-khawātīm): he thus exhorts indirectly to repentance and to refusing despair of mercy. In the morning they swore by Pharaoh; by evening they believed in the Lord of the worlds.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ل م

versets.trad: the Lord of Moses and Aaron!”
versets.commentaire: They specify: the Lord of Moses and Aaron, lest it be thought they mean Pharaoh. Qushayrī sees in this new faith hearts wholly turned toward God: their first concern will at once be the forgiveness of their past sins.

versets.ar: قَالَ ءَامَنتُمْ لَهُۥ قَبْلَ أَنْ ءَاذَنَ لَكُمْ إِنَّهُۥ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱلسِّحْرَ فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُم مِّنْ خِلَٰفٍ وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ
versets.tr: qāla āmantum lahu qabla an ādhana lakum innahu la-kabīrukumu lladhī ʿallamakumu s-siḥra fa-la-sawfa taʿlamūna la-uqaṭṭiʿanna aydiyakum wa-arjulakum min khilāfin wa-la-uṣallibannakum ajmaʿīna
versets.trad: Pharaoh said: “You have believed in him before I gave you leave. He is surely your chief who taught you magic, but soon you shall know! I shall surely cut off your hands and your feet on opposite sides, then I shall crucify you all.”
versets.commentaire: The tyrant threatens with torments those whom faith has just set free. Qushayrī had shown that these new believers did not waver at the threat of death, crucifixion and mutilation: in the morning they swore by Pharaoh, and before evening they said they preferred the truth to all the king's power. The threat no longer grips the liberated heart.

versets.ar: قَالُوا۟ لَا ضَيْرَ إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنقَلِبُونَ
versets.tr: qālū lā ḍayra innā ilā rabbinā munqalibūna
versets.trad: They said: “No harm! Truly to our Lord we shall return.
versets.commentaire: “No harm!”: the believers have conquered the fear of death. Qushayrī teaches that for God's friends death is spoil and grace, for through it they reach Him. Whoever knows his return to the Lord no longer dreads the tyrant's tortures.

versets.ar: إِنَّا نَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لَنَا رَبُّنَا خَطَٰيَٰنَآ أَن كُنَّآ أَوَّلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: innā naṭmaʿu an yaghfira lanā rabbunā khaṭāyānā an kunnā awwala l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: We hope that our Lord will forgive us our sins, since we are the first of the believers.”
versets.commentaire: No sooner converted, their first concern is forgiveness, says Qushayrī: when success aided them and they believed in God, their most pressing business was to implore forgiveness for their past sins — and this is the ultimate aim of God's friends, to seek refuge in Him from His chastisement; the most knowing of God is the most fearful of Him.

versets.ar: وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبَادِىٓ إِنَّكُم مُّتَّبَعُونَ
versets.tr: wa-awḥaynā ilā mūsā an asri bi-ʿibādī innakum muttabaʿūna
versets.trad: And We revealed to Moses: “Set out by night with My servants; you will be pursued.”
versets.commentaire: God commands the nocturnal exodus of Israel. Qushayrī, recalling when God ordered Moses to bring the Children of Israel out, situates here the start of the great rescue: Pharaoh will pursue, but God has already decreed the deliverance of His own and the tyrant's ruin.

versets.trad: Then Pharaoh sent heralds into the cities:
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh mobilizes his troops against the little band. Qushayrī lets the narrative show the blind pride: the tyrant arms himself against fugitives, unaware he is rushing to his own ruin. The strength of numbers is nothing before the divine decree.

versets.trad: “These are but a small band;
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh scorns the small troop. But the weak in men's eyes is the chosen in God's: Qushayrī, concerning the humble followers of the prophets, will recall that the weakest are, in God's decree, the foremost and the most honored.

versets.trad: yet they enrage us,
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh stokes his people's resentment. Qushayrī shows how the oppressor recasts the flight of the oppressed as provocation: it is the logic of the powerful who poses as victim. The decree, however, is about to reverse the roles.

versets.trad: but we are a vigilant multitude.”
versets.commentaire: The tyrant reassures himself by numbers and vigilance. Qushayrī lets the irony of the decree speak: this “vigilant” army will be drowned. Human precaution does not prevail against the One who disposes of the two groups and leads each to its term.

versets.trad: So We drove them out from gardens and springs,
versets.commentaire: God brings the Egyptians out of their gardens and springs: the verse shows the reversal by which the oppressor loses what he thought his own. Qushayrī sees in this stripping the prelude to the gift: these goods will pass to yesterday's oppressed.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ع ي ن

versets.trad: and treasures and a noble station.
versets.commentaire: Treasures and noble dwellings: all that made the tyrant's pride is named the better to mark his loss. For Qushayrī, these riches are the very veil: whoever sleeps in them forgets the Giver, and whoever is stripped of them may at last turn to Him.

mots.racine: ق و م / ك ر م

versets.trad: Thus it was; and We made the Children of Israel inherit them.
versets.commentaire: The tyrant's goods pass to the oppressed: God makes Israel inherit. Qushayrī loves this justice of the decree where the weak receives what the strong loses; God is with those who fear Him: He saves them from every calamity and distinguishes them with every favor.

mots.def: awratha: to pass on as inheritance.

versets.trad: Then they pursued them at sunrise.
versets.commentaire: The pursuers overtake the fugitives at daybreak. Qushayrī places here the narrative's extreme tension: all seems lost for Israel, but it is precisely at the edge of the abyss that Moses's word will shine forth, “My Lord is with me.”

mots.def: ashraqa: to be at morning, eastward.

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا تَرَٰٓءَا ٱلْجَمْعَانِ قَالَ أَصْحَٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّا لَمُدْرَكُونَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā tarāʾā l-jamʿāni qāla aṣḥābu mūsā innā la-mudrakūna
versets.trad: When the two hosts came in sight of each other, the companions of Moses said: “We are overtaken!”
versets.commentaire: At the sight of the enemy, Moses's companions cry: “We are overtaken!” Qushayrī contrasts their terror with the prophet's certainty: fear looks at the appearance of the “two hosts,” faith looks at the One who holds them both in His hand.

mots.def: tarāʾā: to come into mutual sight.

versets.ar: قَالَ كَلَّآ إِنَّ مَعِىَ رَبِّى سَيَهْدِينِ
versets.tr: qāla kallā inna maʿiya rabbī sa-yahdīni
versets.trad: Moses said: “Nay! My Lord is with me; He will guide me.”
versets.commentaire: Spiritual peak of the narrative. Qushayrī comments: it was as he said, for God guided them and saved them, and drowned Pharaoh and his people. And he cites: “Know that God is with those who fear Him” — He delivers them from every calamity and distinguishes them with every favor. Presence (maʿiyya) dissolves fear.

versets.ar: فَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنِ ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَٱنفَلَقَ فَكَانَ كُلُّ فِرْقٍ كَٱلطَّوْدِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ
versets.tr: fa-awḥaynā ilā mūsā ani ḍrib bi-ʿaṣāka l-baḥra fa-nfalaqa fa-kāna kullu firqin ka-ṭ-ṭawdi l-ʿaẓīmi
versets.trad: We revealed to Moses: “Strike the sea with your staff.” Then it split, and each part was like a mighty mountain.
versets.commentaire: The miracle of the opened sea answers Moses's certainty. For Qushayrī, the staff that had become a serpent and swallowed the magic here splits the waters: the same power that turns essences over opens a path of salvation at the heart of the abyss.

mots.racine: ط و د / ع ظ م

versets.trad: And there We brought the others near,
versets.commentaire: God draws the pursuers near the opened trap. Qushayrī sees in this whole unfolding God's praiseworthy way: He draws the tyrant to the very place where his ruin will be accomplished, as He saves His own where they thought to perish.

versets.trad: and We saved Moses and all who were with him.
versets.commentaire: The salvation of the believers fulfills the promise “He will guide me.” Qushayrī had laid it down: He guided them and saved them. The promised divine presence (maʿiyya) translates into concrete deliverance: whoever walks with God crosses the sea.

versets.trad: Then We drowned the others.
versets.commentaire: Pharaoh's drowning seals the narrative. Qushayrī recalls that He drowned Pharaoh and his people and made them vanish. Moses's “Nay!” has been verified: the tyrant's power and numbers were but foam before the decree.

versets.ar: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: inna fī dhālika la-āyatan wa-mā kāna aktharuhum muʾminīna
versets.trad: Truly in that is a sign, yet most of them are not believers.
versets.commentaire: The refrain seals the account of Moses. Qushayrī: the sign is dazzling — sea split, tyrant drowned, believers saved — but the sign is offered only to one who possesses inner sight. The majority see the wonder without receiving its light.

versets.trad: And indeed your Lord is the Mighty, the Merciful.
versets.commentaire: The seal of the account of Moses. Qushayrī: the Mighty, able to drown the oppressor; the Merciful, who saves His own and defers the chastisement of whoever may yet return. Might and mercy embrace in the same Name.

versets.trad: And recite to them the tidings of Abraham.
versets.commentaire: The narrative passes to the Beloved Friend (al-Khalīl). Qushayrī shows Abraham reproving his father and his people, demanding of them the proof of what he blamed in them: why worship what neither hears, sees, profits nor harms? Abraham's way begins with the question that shakes the inner idol.

versets.ar: إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ
versets.tr: idh qāla li-abīhi wa-qawmihi mā taʿbudūna
versets.trad: When he said to his father and his people: “What do you worship?”
versets.commentaire: Abraham opens with a question that forces examination. Qushayrī: he demanded the proof of their worship. To ask “what do you worship?” is not to be ignorant but to awaken: it summons the heart to face squarely the object of its attachment.

versets.ar: قَالُوا۟ نَعْبُدُ أَصْنَامًا فَنَظَلُّ لَهَا عَٰكِفِينَ
versets.tr: qālū naʿbudu aṣnāman fa-naẓallu lahā ʿākifīna
versets.trad: They said: “We worship idols, and to them we remain ever devoted.”
versets.commentaire: They confess their worship without shame, devoted (ʿākifīn) to their idols. Qushayrī brings out the emptiness of this attachment: they cling assiduously to what neither hears nor answers. The ʿukūf of the heart is worth nothing unless turned toward the Living.

versets.trad: He said: “Do they hear you when you call?
versets.commentaire: Abraham presses the proof: do they hear you? Qushayrī sums up his argument: why worship what neither hears, sees, profits nor harms, what neither feels nor perceives? The mute idol itself denounces the vanity of whoever invokes it.

versets.trad: Or do they profit you, or harm you?”
versets.commentaire: Neither profit nor harm: the idol is powerless. Qushayrī articulates here the heart of the Abrahamic critique. What can neither help nor harm deserves neither hope nor fear; only the Lord of the worlds holds good and ill in His hand.

versets.ar: قَالُوا۟ بَلْ وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا كَذَٰلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ
versets.tr: qālū bal wajadnā ābāʾanā kadhālika yafʿalūna
versets.trad: They said: “No, but we found our fathers doing the same.”
versets.commentaire: They have no answer but the imitation of their ancestors. Qushayrī: in their reply they returned only to the imitation of their predecessors. Blind taqlīd is the bulwark of idolatry: one worships by inherited habit, not by tested certainty.

versets.trad: He said: “Have you then considered what you worship,
versets.commentaire: Having established the proof, Abraham, says Qushayrī, passes to the praise of his Master and the rapturous laudation of His description. The “have you considered?” marks the pivot: he leaves refutation for the celebration of the One.

versets.trad: you and your forefathers of old?
versets.commentaire: Abraham includes the most ancient fathers: the antiquity of an error does not make it a truth. Qushayrī marks here the Khalīl's break with the chain of imitation: however venerable the lineage, it does not sanctify the idol.

versets.ar: فَإِنَّهُمْ عَدُوٌّ لِّىٓ إِلَّا رَبَّ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
versets.tr: fa-innahum ʿaduwwun lī illā rabba l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: Surely they are an enemy to me — not so the Lord of the worlds,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī admires Abraham's delicacy: he mentioned them in the briefest way, not saying ‘they are my enemies’ but using the singular — ‘they are an enemy to me’ — then ‘save the Lord of the worlds’: a detached exception, as if he gently turned away from mentioning them so as to reach the mention of God. And now he can no longer fall silent, stringing together “He who... He who...,” for among the marks of love is to multiply the remembrance of the beloved and to turn from all else.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ل م
mots.sens: save the Lord of the worlds

versets.trad: He who created me, and He guides me,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives “He guides me” a mystical sense: Abraham was already guided, but he intends the guidance to come — that He guide him to Himself through Himself. And he generalizes: men, so long as they are absorbed in themselves, do not find from their own souls the way to their Adored; He guides them out of themselves to Him, and they end annihilated in His existence, vanished from their own attributes.

versets.trad: and He who feeds me and gives me drink,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī goes beyond bread and water: Abraham points to no known food nor familiar drink, but to his sustenance through knowledge, and the drink of love — instead of those who stand only by their ordinary food and drink. The heart of the knower is nourished by other than what others are.

versets.trad: and when I am ill, He heals me,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes Abraham's exquisite courtesy: he does not say ‘when He makes me ill,’ out of respect for the adab of speech — he refers the ill to himself and the healing to God. And he glosses further: it is perhaps less a real illness than the “illness” of lovers, who feign sickness to summon the Beloved's visit.

versets.trad: and He who will cause me to die, then bring me to life,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī refers death to God without flinching, for death, for lovers, is spoil and grace, since it brings them to Him through their souls. And by allusion: “He makes me die” by turning from me in the time of His majesty, “then brings me to life” by turning toward me in the time of His favor; He makes me die to myself and brings me to life through Him.

versets.ar: وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لِى خَطِيٓـَٔتِى يَوْمَ ٱلدِّينِ
versets.tr: wa-lladhī aṭmaʿu an yaghfira lī khaṭīʾatī yawma d-dīni
versets.trad: and He whom I hope will forgive me my sin on the Day of Judgment.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives the lovers' “sin” a subtle sense: the sin of the beloved is to contemplate their own trial, to complain of the pangs of burning longing. He cites the verse: “If my very beauties, in which I gloried, become my sins... then tell me: how shall I excuse myself?” Even the lover's virtue becomes, before God, a cause for humility.

versets.ar: رَبِّ هَبْ لِى حُكْمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِى بِٱلصَّٰلِحِينَ
versets.tr: rabbi hab lī ḥukman wa-alḥiqnī bi-ṣ-ṣāliḥīna
versets.trad: My Lord! Grant me wisdom, and join me with the righteous.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reads “grant me judgment” as a judgment over myself, for whoever has no authority over his soul has none over any other. And “join me with the righteous”: that I fulfill Your right, no longer claiming anything by myself apart from Your right. Wisdom begins with the governance of the self.

versets.ar: وَٱجْعَل لِّى لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ فِى ٱلْءَاخِرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-jʿal lī lisāna ṣidqin fī l-ākhirīna
versets.trad: And grant me a tongue of truthfulness among later generations.
versets.commentaire: For the exegetes, “a tongue of truthfulness” is a fair praise upon the tongue of the community of Muḥammad. Qushayrī adds the intimate allusion: “I mention You only through You, and know You only through You”; and: that I mention You in proclaiming Your bounties, and that after my soul is taken, I mention You forever with an everlasting remembrance.

mots.racine: ل س ن / ص د ق

versets.trad: And make me one of the heirs of the Garden of Bliss.
versets.commentaire: Abraham asks for the inheritance of the Garden of Bliss. Qushayrī, in the flow of this prayer, sees the lover who, having settled his account with his soul, turns all his longing toward the abode of nearness. The true bliss (naʿīm) of the Garden will be the vision of the One he has so often named.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ن ع م

versets.ar: وَٱغْفِرْ لِأَبِىٓ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ
versets.tr: wa-ghfir li-abī innahu kāna mina ḍ-ḍāllīna
versets.trad: And forgive my father, for he was among the erring.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes two readings: in the tongue of the scholars, Abraham says this before despairing of his father's faith; in the tongue of allusion, he says it in an overflow of expansion (basṭ), and this is forgiven to such as he. And he warns: the answering of the servant is no debt upon God; and if He does not answer, the servant finds consolation in the very fact of having been able to address Him so — a secret not everyone attains.

versets.trad: And do not disgrace me on the Day they are raised,
themes: not to be put to shame
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives “do not disgrace me” an elite sense: do not humble me by reminding me of my fault, for the contemplation of what comes from the servant is, for the masters of hearts and the people of election, the harshest of chastisements — because to see anything of self still is for tawḥīd to remain tinged with otherness.

versets.ar: يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ
versets.tr: yawma lā yanfaʿu mālun wa-lā banūna
versets.trad: the Day when neither wealth nor sons avail,
versets.commentaire: On the Day of reckoning, neither fortune nor offspring rescue. Qushayrī here prepares the verse of the “sound heart”: every outward support collapses, and there remains to man only what he has brought from within. The sole provision is the state of the heart.

mots.racine: م و ل / ب ن ي

versets.ar: إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى ٱللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ
versets.tr: illā man atā llāha bi-qalbin salīmin
versets.trad: save one who comes to God with a sound heart.”
versets.commentaire: Spiritual summit of the sūra. Qushayrī gathers the definitions of the “sound heart” (qalb salīm): the one that has saved itself from error, then from innovation, then from heedlessness, then from absence, then from the veil, then from any familiarity with other than God, then from the slightest sidelong glance — so many afflictions from which the great have kept safe and by which the small are tried. And more briefly: the sound heart is the one delivered from the will of its own soul.

mots.racine: ق ل ب / س ل م
mots.def: qalb salīm: heart freed of all but God.

versets.trad: And the Garden will be brought near to the godfearing,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī glosses “brought near” (uzlifat): it is drawn close and advanced in time, for what is coming is near, and it is brought present to sight. Just as the Fire will be dragged by chains to the gathering, it is no wonder that the Garden be brought near to the godfearing. Nearness is another name for grace.

versets.trad: and the Hellfire will be displayed to the erring.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “displayed” (burrizat) — made manifest, so the proof be sealed against the deniers; they are presented to the Fire, and shown the dwellings of the wicked, then hurled into it all together, beginning to confess their sins. The unveiling of the Fire is first the forced confession of the denied truth.

versets.ar: وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ
versets.tr: wa-qīla lahum ayna mā kuntum taʿbudūna
versets.trad: And it will be said to them: “Where are those you worshipped
versets.commentaire: They are summoned: where are your idols? Qushayrī sets this scene within the general confession: hurled into the Fire, they acknowledge their faults. The question “where are they?” reveals the absence of any help apart from God on the day when one would need to be helped.

versets.ar: مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ هَلْ يَنصُرُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَنتَصِرُونَ
versets.tr: min dūni llāhi hal yanṣurūnakum aw yantaṣirūna
versets.trad: besides God? Can they help you, or help themselves?”
versets.commentaire: The idols can neither help others nor save themselves. Qushayrī rejoins Abraham's argument at the start of the account: what neither profits nor harms cannot save. On the Day of reckoning, the idol's impotence becomes a crushing evidence.

versets.trad: Then they will be hurled into it, they and the erring,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī had described the scene: they are hurled into it all together (yukabkabūn) and begin to confess their sins. The pell-mell fall of the idols and their worshippers shows that the object of false attachment drags into its ruin whoever has given himself to it.

versets.trad: and the hosts of Iblis, all together.
versets.commentaire: The tempter's troops sink with their dupes. Qushayrī, in this scene of confession, shows that the leaders of error share the fate of those they led astray. The alliance in revolt turns into companionship in chastisement.

versets.trad: They will say, as they dispute therein:
versets.commentaire: In the Fire, they quarrel. Qushayrī here prepares the most crushing confession: those who allied in idolatry tear at one another in regret. The quarrel of the damned is the reverse of the concord of hearts united in God.

versets.ar: تَٱللَّهِ إِن كُنَّا لَفِى ضَلَٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ
versets.tr: ta-llāhi in kunnā la-fī ḍalālin mubīnin
versets.trad: “By God, we were in manifest error,
versets.commentaire: Too late, they swear by God their past error. Qushayrī moves to the next verse: no shame is uglier, no defect more odious than what they confess against themselves in admitting they were astray. The Fire's confession redeems nothing: it only reveals the long-refused evidence.

mots.racine: ض ل ل / ب ي ن

versets.trad: when we equated you with the Lord of the worlds.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī here names the height of blasphemy: “when we equated you with the Lord of the worlds” — there is no worse door of associationism, no fouler kind of unbelief, no worse state than to liken the creature to the Adored in His description. To set the idol on a level with God is the root of all perdition.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ل م
mots.sens: with the Lord of the worlds

versets.trad: None led us astray but the criminals. So now
versets.commentaire: They cast the blame on the criminals who led them. Qushayrī, in this scene of confession, shows that the strayed one seeks a culprit but only confirms his own loss. Recrimination does not undo the responsibility of the heart that has followed.

versets.trad: we have no intercessors,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī illustrates this verse with a report: on the Day of judgment, a servant whose good and evil deeds balance needs but a single good deed; God tells him: seek among the people, perhaps one will give you one. He goes to his father, mother, friends — and each answers: “Today I myself am in need of a single good deed.” None has an intercessor when each trembles for himself.

versets.trad: nor any devoted friend.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī finishes his report: God reminds the destitute servant that he had a sincere friend “in Me”; he finds him, the friend offers him some of his worship, and God forgives both. Such is the hidden sense of “no devoted friend”: on the Day of destitution, only the friend bound in God can still give. Friendship for God's sake survives where every carnal tie is severed.

versets.ar: فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: fa-law anna lanā karratan fa-nakūna mina l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: If only we had a return, that we might be among the believers!”
versets.commentaire: They dream of a return so as to believe at last. Qushayrī marks the vanity of this wish: the time for faith was life, not the Fire. The “if only” of the damned is the confession that the door open in this world they let close.

versets.ar: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: inna fī dhālika la-āyatan wa-mā kāna aktharuhum muʾminīna
versets.trad: Truly in that is a sign, yet most of them are not believers.
versets.commentaire: The refrain seals the account of Abraham and the scene of Judgment. Qushayrī: the sign is manifest to whoever has clear sight, but most remain veiled. The lesson of the “sound heart” stays on offer; rare are those who receive it.

versets.trad: And indeed your Lord is the Mighty, the Merciful.
versets.commentaire: The seal of Abraham's account. Qushayrī: the Mighty who could annihilate the deniers; the Merciful who defers the chastisement and does not cut off sustenance despite the foulness of deeds. His power suffers nothing from their disobedience, nor does His glory grow by their obedience.

versets.trad: The people of Noah denied the messengers.
versets.commentaire: The procession of prophets opens. To deny Noah is to deny all the messengers, for the message is one. Qushayrī recalls that all those mentioned in this sūra's verses — ʿĀd, Thamūd, the people of Lot, the folk of Madian — met their messengers with denial, and God annihilated them all, and aided His messenger according to His praiseworthy way.

versets.ar: إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ نُوحٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ
versets.tr: idh qāla lahum akhūhum nūḥun a-lā tattaqūna
versets.trad: When their brother Noah said to them: “Will you not fear God?
versets.commentaire: Noah is “their brother”: close to them, he exhorts them to fear. Qushayrī notes that every prophet's call begins with “will you not fear?”: taqwā is the door by which the heart opens to truth, and the refusal to fear is the root of denial.

versets.trad: I am to you a trustworthy messenger.
versets.commentaire: “A trustworthy messenger” (amīn): trustworthiness is the prophet's seal, as the trustworthy Spirit (al-rūḥ al-amīn) bears the Revelation. Qushayrī will later see in this amāna the link between heaven and heart; here, Noah offers his people the guarantee of his word.

mots.racine: ر س ل / أ م ن

versets.trad: So fear God and obey me!
versets.commentaire: The double command — fear God, obey the messenger — sums up all prophethood. Qushayrī sets obedience to the prophet within obedience to God: to follow the trustworthy messenger is to answer the One who sends him. Fear without obedience stays barren.

versets.ar: وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā asʾalukum ʿalayhi min ajrin in ajriya illā ʿalā rabbi l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: I ask of you no wage for it; my wage is only with the Lord of the worlds.
versets.commentaire: Refrain of all the prophets: no wage demanded of men. Qushayrī makes it a lesson for the scholars, heirs of the prophets: let them ask the people nothing for spreading their knowledge; whoever sells the reminder of religion, God removes the blessing from what the people hear of it and from what the scholar receives — he sells his religion for a paltry price and incurs God's wrath.

versets.trad: So fear God and obey me!”
versets.commentaire: Noah repeats the call: prophetic pedagogy insists. Qushayrī sees in this repetition the patient love of the messenger who never tires of inviting; the reminder is said again because the heart needs ceaselessly to be brought back to fear and obedience.

versets.trad: They said: “Shall we believe in you when the basest follow you?”
versets.commentaire: They scorn Noah's humble disciples. Qushayrī overturns their judgment: the followers of every messenger are the weakest, but they are, in God's decree, the foremost and the noblest. He cites the Prophet: “I am aided by your weak ones.” In heaven's eyes, the despised is the chosen.

versets.ar: قَالَ وَمَا عِلْمِى بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ
versets.tr: qāla wa-mā ʿilmī bi-mā kānū yaʿmalūna
versets.trad: He said: “What knowledge have I of what they used to do?
versets.commentaire: Noah refuses to judge his followers by their social rank: their account belongs to God. Qushayrī, faithful to his reading of the humble elect, shows that the prophet does not measure the worth of souls by their station; the Real alone knows the secret of hearts.

versets.ar: إِنْ حِسَابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّى لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ
versets.tr: in ḥisābuhum illā ʿalā rabbī law tashʿurūna
versets.trad: Their reckoning is only with my Lord, if you but perceived.
versets.commentaire: The judgment of hearts belongs to God alone. Qushayrī holds that rank before God does not depend on rank before men: the weak may be the foremost. To refer the reckoning to the Lord is to refuse to drive away believers because of their humble appearance.

versets.trad: I am not one to drive away the believers;
versets.commentaire: Noah refuses to drive away the humble believers to please the notables. Qushayrī echoes the command given to the Prophet: lower your wing to the believers who follow you. The prophet welcomes the weak; to drive them off for the powerful would be to betray the mission.

versets.trad: I am only a clear warner.”
versets.commentaire: Noah defines himself: nothing but a clear warner. Qushayrī recalls that the messenger's duty is clear conveyance, not compulsion nor judgment. Explicit warning (nadhīr mubīn) leaves to each the responsibility of his response.

versets.ar: قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ يَٰنُوحُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَرْجُومِينَ
versets.tr: qālū la-in lam tantahi yā nūḥu la-takūnanna mina l-marjūmīna
versets.trad: They said: “If you do not desist, O Noah, you shall surely be stoned!”
versets.commentaire: As Pharaoh threatened Moses with prison, they threaten Noah with stoning. Qushayrī shows the deniers' constant: lacking argument, they resort to violence. The threat is the confession that the prophet's proof has reduced them to silence.

versets.trad: He said: “My Lord! My people have called me a liar.
versets.commentaire: Noah does not avenge himself: he carries his plaint to God. Qushayrī sees in this recourse the way of the oppressed servant who entrusts his cause to the Lord. Faced with denial, the prophet turns not to retaliation but to the One who will decide.

versets.ar: فَٱفْتَحْ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحًا وَنَجِّنِى وَمَن مَّعِىَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: fa-ftaḥ baynī wa-baynahum fatḥan wa-najjinī wa-man maʿiya mina l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: So decide clearly between me and them, and save me and the believers who are with me.”
versets.commentaire: Noah asks for arbitration and the salvation of the believers. Qushayrī, who reports that God drowned the obstinate people, sees here the prayer answered: “decide between us” calls for the judgment that separates the saved from the lost. The deliverance of the faithful accompanies the perishing of the denier.

versets.ar: فَأَنجَيْنَٰهُ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ
versets.tr: fa-anjaynāhu wa-man maʿahu fī l-fulki l-mashḥūni
versets.trad: So We saved him and those with him in the laden ship.
versets.commentaire: The laden ark (al-fulk al-mashḥūn) saves the believers from the waters. Qushayrī makes the flood the image of the decree: God delivers His own in the vessel of His keeping while the sea swallows the obstinate. Salvation always has its ship for whoever embarks with the prophet.

mots.racine: ف ل ك / ش ح ن

versets.trad: Then We drowned the rest.
versets.commentaire: The obstinate are engulfed. Qushayrī laid it down: God drowned his people when they persisted and swelled with pride. The flood seals the fate of whoever refused the ark: the decree distinguishes forever those who boarded and those who stayed.

versets.ar: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: inna fī dhālika la-āyatan wa-mā kāna aktharuhum muʾminīna
versets.trad: Truly in that is a sign, yet most of them are not believers.
versets.commentaire: The refrain seals the account of Noah. Qushayrī: the flood is a sign for whoever sees, but most remain veiled. Each story repeats the same lesson: the sign never fails — it is the gaze of faith that is lacking.

versets.trad: And indeed your Lord is the Mighty, the Merciful.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī comments on the seal: the Mighty (al-ʿAzīz), able to exterminate them; the Merciful (al-Raḥīm), who deferred the chastisement by granting them respite and did not cut off their sustenance despite the foulness of their deeds. His power does not suffer from their evil, nor would His glory be adorned by their obedience.

versets.trad: ʿĀd denied the messengers,
versets.commentaire: ʿĀd deny in their turn. Qushayrī ranks this people among those who met their messengers with denial, and God annihilated them all. To deny a messenger is to deny the very principle of divine sending: the refrain of denial heralds the refrain of chastisement.

versets.ar: إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ
versets.tr: idh qāla lahum akhūhum hūdun a-lā tattaqūna
versets.trad: when their brother Hūd said to them: “Will you not fear God?
versets.commentaire: Hūd, their brother, takes up the call to fear. Qushayrī stresses the unity of the message: each prophet begins with “will you not fear?” Taqwā is the common threshold by which every heart enters truth, in every age.

versets.trad: I am to you a trustworthy messenger.
versets.commentaire: Hūd offers the same guarantee: a trustworthy messenger. Qushayrī makes amāna the common mark of the prophets; the bearer's trustworthiness authenticates the message. The constancy of this formula speaks the unity of prophethood across the ages.

mots.racine: ر س ل / أ م ن

versets.trad: So fear God and obey me.
versets.commentaire: The same double command. Qushayrī holds obedience to the prophet as the concrete form of the fear of God. Repeated from Noah to Hūd, the formula engraves in the heart that the way is one: to fear the One and to follow His messenger.

versets.ar: وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā asʾalukum ʿalayhi min ajrin in ajriya illā ʿalā rabbi l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: I ask of you no wage for it; my wage is only with the Lord of the worlds.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes this verse, ascribed to each prophet, a golden rule: God reports of each of them: “I ask of you no wage,” so all may know that whoever works for God should not ask a wage of another. A warning to the scholars, heirs of the prophets: let them school themselves by their prophets' example and ask the people nothing for transmitting their knowledge.

versets.trad: Do you build on every hill a monument, sporting?
versets.commentaire: Hūd denounces the monuments raised in sport on every height. Qushayrī, attentive to the heart, sees in them the passion to be seen and the forgetting of the end: to build “for amusement” is the sign of a soul that sports with meaning, erecting empty signs instead of reading God's signs.

versets.trad: and take for yourselves strongholds, as if you would abide forever?
versets.commentaire: They build as if they would never die. Qushayrī touches the heart of error: to forget death is to think oneself eternal on earth. The fortresses of the body do not hold back the summoned soul; the illusion of immortality is the veil of the Day when neither goods nor buildings avail.

versets.trad: And when you seize, you seize as tyrants?
versets.commentaire: Hūd reproaches the tyrannical violence of their power. Qushayrī opposes to this harshness the lowering of the wing enjoined upon the Prophet: force that strikes without mercy (jabbār) is the reverse of the believer's gentleness. Power becomes chastisement when it forgets clemency.

versets.trad: So fear God and obey me!
versets.commentaire: Hūd returns to the call after the reproach. Qushayrī sees in this repetition the prophet's patience who, having shown the evil, always brings back to the remedy: to fear God and obey. The reminder is said again because the hardened heart needs ceaselessly to be awakened.

versets.trad: And fear the One who has supplied you with what you know:
versets.commentaire: Hūd calls to fear the very Giver of their goods. Qushayrī sets fear within gratitude: He who provides deserves to be feared out of thankfulness. To forget that all comes from Him is the first step of ingratitude.

versets.trad: He has supplied you with cattle and sons,
versets.commentaire: Cattle and sons: the gifts that make man's pride. Qushayrī recalls elsewhere that wealth and children will not avail on the Day of reckoning; here Hūd names them as so many grounds for gratitude that should lead to fear, not to pride.

versets.trad: and gardens and springs.
versets.commentaire: Gardens and springs complete the enumeration of graces. Qushayrī invites us to read in these gifts the signs of the Provider: whoever beholds spring and garden and recognizes the Giver is led to fear; whoever sees in them only enjoyment falls asleep in forgetting.

versets.ar: إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ
versets.tr: innī akhāfu ʿalaykum ʿadhāba yawmin ʿaẓīmin
versets.trad: Truly I fear for you the chastisement of a mighty Day.”
versets.commentaire: Hūd fears for them the chastisement of a great Day. Qushayrī sees in this fear of the prophet the love that worries over another's salvation: he dreads for them what they do not dread for themselves. The messenger's compassion watches where the hardened heart goes blind.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / ع ظ م

versets.ar: قَالُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْنَآ أَوَعَظْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْوَٰعِظِينَ
versets.tr: qālū sawāʾun ʿalaynā a-waʿaẓta am lam takun mina l-wāʿiẓīna
versets.trad: They retorted: “It is all one to us whether you admonish or are not of the admonishers.
versets.commentaire: Their heart is dead to the reminder: admonition or silence are alike to them. Qushayrī sees here the height of the veil, when the ear hears without the heart receiving — indifference is graver than opposition, for it refuses even the possibility of being touched.

versets.trad: This is nothing but the way of the ancients, and
versets.commentaire: They shelter behind the customs of the ancients. Qushayrī, as with Abraham's people, denounces the taqlīd that serves as a rampart for unbelief: to invoke the custom of forefathers to reject the reminder is to prefer inherited habit to present truth.

versets.trad: we shall not be chastised.”
versets.commentaire: They deny any chastisement to come. Qushayrī sees in this denial the blindness that precedes the fall: to believe one will escape the reckoning is the illusion of the heedless soul. The next verse will seal their fate: destruction answers denial.

versets.ar: فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَهْلَكْنَٰهُمْ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: fa-kadhdhabūhu fa-ahlaknāhum inna fī dhālika la-āyatan wa-mā kāna aktharuhum muʾminīna
versets.trad: So they denied him, and We destroyed them. Truly in that is a sign, yet most of them are not believers.
versets.commentaire: Denial then destruction: the way repeats itself. Qushayrī: each of these peoples met its messenger with denial, and God annihilated them all, and aided His messenger. The sign is offered even in the ruin, but most remain veiled.

versets.trad: And indeed your Lord is the Mighty, the Merciful.
versets.commentaire: The seal of Hūd's account. Qushayrī: the Mighty who could annihilate them with a breath; the Merciful who had granted them respite and sustenance. The same Name embraces the rigor that chastises and the gentleness that waits.

versets.trad: Thamūd denied the messengers.
versets.commentaire: Thamūd deny in their turn. Qushayrī ranks them among the peoples who met their messengers with denial and whom God annihilated. The repetition of the same verb — kadhdhabat — from people to people speaks the recurring obstinacy of the heart closed to truth across history.

versets.ar: إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ صَٰلِحٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ
versets.tr: idh qāla lahum akhūhum ṣāliḥun a-lā tattaqūna
versets.trad: When their brother Ṣāliḥ said to them: “Will you not fear God?
versets.commentaire: Ṣāliḥ, their brother, launches the same call to fear. Qushayrī stresses the prophet's brotherhood with his people: close to them, he exhorts them out of love. Taqwā remains the one door offered to each generation.

versets.trad: I am to you a trustworthy messenger.
versets.commentaire: Ṣāliḥ presents himself as a trustworthy messenger. Qushayrī makes amāna the common seal of the prophets: trustworthiness authenticates the mission. This formula, repeated from one messenger to the next, engraves the unity of prophethood in attentive hearts.

mots.racine: ر س ل / أ م ن

versets.trad: So fear God and obey me!
versets.commentaire: The same double command. Qushayrī recalls that obedience to the prophet is the concrete face of the fear of God. The constant formula weaves, from Noah to Ṣāliḥ, the fabric of one and the same way.

versets.ar: وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā asʾalukum ʿalayhi min ajrin in ajriya illā ʿalā rabbi l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: I ask of you no wage for it; my wage is only with the Lord of the worlds.
versets.commentaire: Ṣāliḥ asks no wage. Qushayrī holds this refrain a lesson for the scholars: let them ask the people nothing for transmitting their knowledge; whoever works for God expects reward from God alone. The prophet's disinterestedness is the seal of authenticity.

versets.trad: Will you be left here secure
versets.commentaire: Ṣāliḥ rouses them from their false security. Qushayrī touches the heart of the illusion: to think oneself safe in earthly enjoyment is the sleep of the forgetful soul. None is “left secure” here below; the quiet of goods is a veil over the imminence of the reckoning.

versets.trad: amid gardens and springs,
versets.commentaire: Gardens and springs, fleeting gifts. Qushayrī invites us to read these graces as signs of the Provider, not as guarantees of eternity. Whoever sleeps in them forgets the Giver; whoever contemplates them lucidly sees in them the call to gratitude and fear.

versets.trad: and crops and palm trees with slender fruit-stalks,
versets.commentaire: Crops and palms with heavy clusters: the abundance that lulls. Qushayrī recalls that these goods, like cattle and children, will not avail on the Day of reckoning. Ṣāliḥ names them to awaken gratitude, not to flatter attachment.

versets.trad: and you carve houses out of the mountains with skill?
versets.commentaire: They carve houses from the rock with pride and skill. Qushayrī, attentive to the heart, sees in them the soul that fortifies itself against death and thinks itself immortal in stone. To carve dwellings “with arrogance” (fārihīn) is the gesture of one who forgets that no mountain holds back the summoned soul.

versets.trad: So fear God and obey me.
versets.commentaire: Ṣāliḥ returns to the call after the warning. Qushayrī sees in this repetition the prophet's loving constancy: to show the danger, then bring back to the remedy. Fear and obedience remain the one issue offered to the heart.

versets.trad: And do not obey the command of the prodigal,
versets.commentaire: Ṣāliḥ warns against obeying the excessive. Qushayrī opposes obedience to the prophet to obedience to the leaders of excess: to follow the corruptors is to betray the way. The free heart discerns to whom it lends its obedience.

versets.ar: ٱلَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ
versets.tr: alladhīna yufsidūna fī l-arḍi wa-lā yuṣliḥūna
versets.trad: who spread corruption on the earth and do not reform.”
versets.commentaire: The corruptors sow disorder without ever reforming. Qushayrī, who places the rectification of self (iṣlāḥ) at the threshold of wisdom, here denounces the soul that destroys without building. To corrupt without reforming is the exact opposite of the way of the righteous.

versets.trad: They said: “You are only one of the bewitched;
versets.commentaire: As Thamūd to Ṣāliḥ, they accuse him of being bewitched. Qushayrī shows the monotony of denial: the deniers take up the same insults from people to people. To call the prophet bewitched is the constant evasion of one who refuses to examine the proof.

versets.ar: مَآ أَنتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا فَأْتِ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّٰدِقِينَ
versets.tr: mā anta illā basharun mithlunā fa-ʾti bi-āyatin in kunta mina ṣ-ṣādiqīna
versets.trad: you are only a mortal like us. So bring a sign, if you are of the truthful!”
versets.commentaire: They object that the prophet is but a mortal like them. Qushayrī, throughout the sūra, shows that the messenger's humanity takes nothing from his mission; to refuse to believe “because he is a man like us” is the pride that cannot conceive God choosing from among men.

mots.racine: ب ش ر / م ث ل

versets.ar: قَالَ هَٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةٌ لَّهَا شِرْبٌ وَلَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ
versets.tr: qāla hādhihi nāqatun lahā shirbun wa-lakum shirbu yawmin maʿlūmin
versets.trad: He said: “This is a she-camel: hers is a turn to drink, and yours a turn on a known day.
versets.commentaire: Ṣāliḥ produces the sign: the she-camel with a shared turn at the water. Qushayrī, who refers the sign to the divine power of turning essences over, sees in this trial a test of the heart: to respect the camel's turn is to acknowledge God's lordship over the order of things.

versets.ar: وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ
versets.tr: wa-lā tamassūhā bi-sūʾin fa-yaʾkhudhakum ʿadhābu yawmin ʿaẓīmin
versets.trad: And do not touch her with harm, lest the chastisement of a mighty Day seize you.”
versets.commentaire: Ṣāliḥ forbids touching the she-camel on pain of chastisement. Qushayrī sees in this prohibition the test of obedience: the sign is not only to be seen, it is to be respected. To transgress the prohibition is to call down upon oneself the chastisement of the great Day.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / ع ظ م

versets.trad: But they hamstrung her, and then became regretful,
versets.commentaire: They slay the she-camel and regret comes too late. Qushayrī teaches that what counts is the endings; but this late repentance, arising at the imminence of chastisement, is no longer the kind that saves. Remorse that does not precede the fault but follows it under threat redeems nothing.

versets.ar: فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: fa-akhadhahumu l-ʿadhābu inna fī dhālika la-āyatan wa-mā kāna aktharuhum muʾminīna
versets.trad: for the chastisement seized them. Truly in that is a sign, yet most of them are not believers.
versets.commentaire: The chastisement strikes; the refrain seals the account. Qushayrī: the divine way aids the messenger and annihilates the obstinate people. The sign remains offered even in the ruin, but most stay veiled to the lesson.

versets.trad: And indeed your Lord is the Mighty, the Merciful.
versets.commentaire: The seal of Ṣāliḥ's account. Qushayrī: the Mighty who could seize them; the Merciful who had left them a respite before the crime. Rigor and patience meet in the same Name, as justice and mercy in the same decree.

versets.trad: The people of Lot denied the messengers.
versets.commentaire: The people of Lot deny in their turn. Qushayrī ranks them among the peoples chastised for having met their messengers with denial. The verb repeats, tireless, from one account to the next: the closed heart reproduces always the same refusal across the ages.

versets.ar: إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ لُوطٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ
versets.tr: idh qāla lahum akhūhum lūṭun a-lā tattaqūna
versets.trad: When their brother Lot said to them: “Will you not fear God?
versets.commentaire: Lot, their brother, takes up the call to fear. Qushayrī again stresses the unity of the message: “will you not fear?” opens every mission. Taqwā is the first step, refused by each obstinate people.

versets.trad: I am to you a trustworthy messenger.
versets.commentaire: Lot presents himself as a trustworthy messenger. Qushayrī makes amāna the shared seal of the prophets; the bearer's trustworthiness guarantees the truth of the message. The constancy of the formula speaks the unity of the prophetic way.

mots.racine: ر س ل / أ م ن

versets.trad: So fear God and obey me.
versets.commentaire: The same double command. Qushayrī holds obedience to the prophet as the concrete face of the fear of God. From Noah to Lot, the formula engraves in the heart the unity of one way: to fear the One, to follow His messenger.

versets.ar: وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā asʾalukum ʿalayhi min ajrin in ajriya illā ʿalā rabbi l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: I ask of you no wage for it; my wage is only with the Lord of the worlds.
versets.commentaire: Lot asks no wage. Qushayrī repeats his lesson: whoever works for God expects reward only from God, and the scholars, heirs of the prophets, must take inspiration from this by not selling the reminder. Disinterestedness authenticates the mission.

versets.trad: Of all creatures, do you approach males,
versets.commentaire: Lot denounces the transgression of the created order. Qushayrī, who grounds the way on respect for the nature willed by God, sees in this aberration the overturning of the Creator's order: to forsake that for which one was created is to depart from rectitude (istiqāma).

versets.ar: وَتَذَرُونَ مَا خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ عَادُونَ
versets.tr: wa-tadharūna mā khalaqa lakum rabbukum min azwājikum bal antum qawmun ʿādūna
versets.trad: and forsake the spouses your Lord created for you? Indeed you are a transgressing people!”
versets.commentaire: Lot reproaches them with forsaking what the Lord created for them. Qushayrī sees the sin in this overturning: to abandon the order willed by the Creator for transgression. “A transgressing people” (ʿādūn): those who overstep the limit God set to nature.

versets.ar: قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ يَٰلُوطُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُخْرَجِينَ
versets.tr: qālū la-in lam tantahi yā lūṭu la-takūnanna mina l-mukhrajīna
versets.trad: They said: “If you do not desist, O Lot, you shall surely be expelled!”
versets.commentaire: As Noah was threatened with stoning, Lot is threatened with expulsion. Qushayrī shows the deniers' constant: lacking argument, they resort to exclusion. To drive away the prophet who recalls rectitude is the confession that his word has stung them to the quick.

versets.trad: He said: “Truly I am of those who detest your deed.
versets.commentaire: Lot expresses his total revulsion for their conduct. Qushayrī, who enjoins on the heart hatred of evil as much as love of good, sees in this loathing (qilā) the virtue of the prophet who does not compromise with corruption. To detest the act without hating the person is the measure of the just.

versets.trad: My Lord! Save me and my family from what they do.”
versets.commentaire: Lot asks for salvation for himself and his own. Qushayrī sees in this recourse the way of the just one beset by evil: he fights not by force but entrusts his cause to God. The prophet's prayer for his family calls forth the deliverance God will grant.

versets.trad: So We saved him and all his family,
versets.commentaire: God saves Lot and all his own. Qushayrī sees here the answered prayer and the way of salvation: God delivers His own from the corrupt milieu. The prophet's family, save for the exception, is carried out of the ruin.

versets.trad: except an old woman among those who stayed behind;
versets.commentaire: One alone stays behind: the complicit wife. Qushayrī, who showed for Noah and Abraham that kinship does not save without faith, finds here the same lesson: the carnal bond does not wrench from perdition the one whose heart has remained with the deniers.

versets.trad: then We destroyed the rest.
versets.commentaire: The rest are annihilated. Qushayrī laid it down: God destroyed them all, those who persisted. The way repeats: the salvation of the faithful goes with the perishing of the obstinate, and the justice of the decree distinguishes the two groups forever.

versets.ar: وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا فَسَآءَ مَطَرُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ
versets.tr: wa-amṭarnā ʿalayhim maṭaran fa-sāʾa maṭaru l-mundharīna
versets.trad: And We rained upon them a rain — and evil was the rain of those who had been warned!
versets.commentaire: A rain of stones falls upon them. Qushayrī stresses that the chastisement comes after the warning: “evil was the rain of those who had been warned!” The messenger had forewarned them; ruin falls only upon whoever refused to hear.

versets.ar: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: inna fī dhālika la-āyatan wa-mā kāna aktharuhum muʾminīna
versets.trad: Truly in that is a sign, yet most of them are not believers.
versets.commentaire: The refrain seals the account of Lot. Qushayrī: the sign is offered to whoever has clear sight, but most remain veiled. The evil rain, like the flood and the engulfing, remains a sign that only the believing heart deciphers.

versets.trad: And indeed your Lord is the Mighty, the Merciful.
versets.commentaire: The seal of Lot's account. Qushayrī: the Mighty who rained down the ruin; the Merciful who saved the just one and his own. The same Name bears the rigor of chastisement and the gentleness of deliverance.

versets.trad: The people of the Thicket denied the messengers.
themes: the denial of the people of the Thicket
versets.commentaire: The people of the Thicket (al-Ayka) deny the last prophet of the series. Qushayrī counts them among the peoples who met their messengers with denial. The procession ends as it began: in refusal, the inevitable prelude to chastisement.

mots.racine: ص ح ب / أ ي ك

versets.ar: إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ شُعَيْبٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ
versets.tr: idh qāla lahum shuʿaybun a-lā tattaqūna
versets.trad: When Shuʿayb said to them: “Will you not fear God?
