versets.commentaire: Shuʿayb takes up the call to fear. Qushayrī notes that, alone of the series, he is not called “their brother,” for he was not of their lineage; yet the call remains identical. Taqwā is the one door, beyond the ties of blood.

versets.trad: I am to you a trustworthy messenger.
versets.commentaire: Shuʿayb presents himself as a trustworthy messenger. Qushayrī makes amāna the seal of all the prophets; here it takes on a special relief, for Shuʿayb will call his people to honesty in dealings. The bearer's trustworthiness prepares the call to uprightness.

mots.racine: ر س ل / أ م ن

versets.trad: So fear God and obey me.
versets.commentaire: The same double command. Qushayrī holds obedience to the prophet as the concrete form of the fear of God. The formula, repeated down to the last prophet of the series, seals the unity of the way offered to all peoples.

versets.ar: وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَٰلَمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā asʾalukum ʿalayhi min ajrin in ajriya illā ʿalā rabbi l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: I ask of you no wage for it; my wage is only with the Lord of the worlds.
versets.commentaire: Shuʿayb asks no wage. Qushayrī repeats his golden rule: whoever works for God expects reward only from God. That one who calls to honesty be himself disinterested authenticates his message; the prophet's coherence seals his word.

versets.ar: أَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُخْسِرِينَ
versets.tr: awfū l-kayla wa-lā takūnū mina l-mukhsirīna
versets.trad: Give full measure, and be not of those who give short measure;
versets.commentaire: Shuʿayb calls for full measure. Qushayrī sees in honesty of dealings the extension of the heart's sincerity: to cheat the measure is to betray trust, like the soul that cheats itself. Rectitude begins at the just weight.

mots.racine: و ف ي / ك ي ل

mots.def: mukhsir: one who causes loss, who skimps.

versets.trad: and weigh with the true balance.
versets.commentaire: To weigh with the upright balance (al-qisṭās al-mustaqīm). Qushayrī, who places istiqāma at the heart of the way, sees in the just balance the image of inner uprightness: the man upright in his weighing is upright also in his heart. Outward equity reflects inner sincerity.

mots.racine: ق س ط / ق و م

versets.ar: وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ
versets.tr: wa-lā tabkhasū n-nāsa ashyāʾahum wa-lā taʿthaw fī l-arḍi mufsidīna
versets.trad: and do not defraud people of their goods, nor spread corruption on the earth.
versets.commentaire: Shuʿayb forbids wronging others and corrupting the earth. Qushayrī unites justice toward men and respect for the divine order: to despoil one's neighbor and to sow disorder are the same rupture of the equity willed by God. Rectitude is indivisible.

versets.trad: And fear the One who created you and the former generations.”
versets.commentaire: Shuʿayb calls to fear the Creator of all, ancient and present. Qushayrī ties fear to creation: He who drew you from nothing, and your ancestors before you, deserves to be feared. To acknowledge the Creator is to submit to His measure in every act.

mots.racine: ج ب ل / أ و ل

versets.trad: They said: “You are only one of the bewitched,
versets.commentaire: As Thamūd to Ṣāliḥ, they accuse Shuʿayb of being bewitched. Qushayrī shows the monotony of denial: the deniers take up the same insults from people to people. To call the prophet enchanted is the constant evasion of one who refuses to examine the proof.

versets.ar: وَمَآ أَنتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا وَإِن نَّظُنُّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْكَٰذِبِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā anta illā basharun mithlunā wa-in naẓunnuka la-mina l-kādhibīna
versets.trad: and you are only a mortal like us. Indeed we think you are a liar.
versets.commentaire: They reduce Shuʿayb to a mere mortal and suspect him of lying. Qushayrī shows that pride cannot conceive God choosing a man “like us”: the prophet's humanity, far from invalidating his mission, is precisely the mode by which God speaks to men.

mots.racine: ب ش ر / م ث ل

versets.ar: فَأَسْقِطْ عَلَيْنَا كِسَفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّٰدِقِينَ
versets.tr: fa-asqiṭ ʿalaynā kisafan mina s-samāʾi in kunta mina ṣ-ṣādiqīna
versets.trad: So make fragments of the sky fall upon us, if you are of the truthful!”
versets.commentaire: They dare Shuʿayb to bring the sky down upon them. Qushayrī shows the blindness of the challenge: to demand chastisement in bravado is to ignore that it will come unawares. The “if you are truthful” hurled in mockery will turn into the “Day of the Shadow” that seizes them.

versets.trad: He said: “My Lord knows best what you do.”
versets.commentaire: Shuʿayb refers their judgment to God's knowledge. Qushayrī sees in this answer the serenity of the prophet who does not take the chastisement upon himself: “My Lord knows best what you do.” To refer to the Witness of all is the attitude of the servant who does not arrogate vengeance.

versets.ar: فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمِ ٱلظُّلَّةِ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ
versets.tr: fa-kadhdhabūhu fa-akhadhahum ʿadhābu yawmi ẓ-ẓullati innahu kāna ʿadhāba yawmin ʿaẓīmin
versets.trad: So they denied him, and the chastisement of the Day of the Shadow seized them: it was the chastisement of a mighty Day.
versets.commentaire: The chastisement of the “Day of the Shadow” seizes them. Qushayrī: the divine way is accomplished, denial followed by ruin. The shadow they thought a relief was the veil of fire; the “mighty Day” they defied struck them at the moment they least expected.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / ظ ل ل
mots.sens: chastisement of the Day of the Shadow

mots.racine: ي و م / ع ظ م

versets.ar: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَءَايَةً وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: inna fī dhālika la-āyatan wa-mā kāna aktharuhum muʾminīna
versets.trad: Truly in that is a sign, yet most of them are not believers.
versets.commentaire: The refrain seals the last account of the procession. Qushayrī: the sign is offered to whoever sees, but most remain veiled. The repetition of this refrain through all the prophets engraves the one lesson: the sign never fails, it is faith that is lacking.

versets.trad: And indeed your Lord is the Mighty, the Merciful.
versets.commentaire: The final seal of the procession of prophets. Qushayrī: the Mighty who could annihilate them all; the Merciful who granted each respite and sustenance. The double Name, refrain after refrain, embraces rigor and mercy in one and the same Lord.

versets.trad: Truly it is a revelation from the Lord of the worlds.
versets.commentaire: The Qurʾān is a descent from the Lord of the worlds. Qushayrī: the Word of God, the Mighty, is in truth sent down upon the Messenger's heart through the agency of Gabriel; the Word is not separate from God nor joined to other than Him — and it is sent down in the proper sense, not the figurative. The Word comes from the Real, intact.

mots.def: tanzīl: that which is sent down.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ع ل م

versets.trad: The trustworthy Spirit (Gabriel) has brought it down upon your heart,
versets.commentaire: “The trustworthy Spirit” (al-rūḥ al-amīn) is Gabriel. Qushayrī explains the mode of descent: Gabriel was in heaven, he heard from the Lord, retained, descended and conveyed to the Messenger. At times a state would seize him at the reception of the revelation, then Gabriel would lay it upon his heart; at times the angel would appear to him and he would hear him. The bearer's amāna guarantees the integrity of the deposit.

mots.racine: ر و ح / أ م ن
mots.def: rūḥ amīn: Gabriel, the faithful bearer.

versets.trad: that you may be one of the warners,
versets.commentaire: The Revelation descends “upon your heart.” Qushayrī makes the Prophet's heart the very place where the Word is laid down: not the ear alone, but the center of being. The Messenger retains it and conveys it; God guarantees him that he will recite it without forgetting, gathering the memory in his heart and easing the recitation for him.

versets.trad: in a clear Arabic tongue.
versets.commentaire: The Word descends in clear Arabic. Qushayrī, having established that men were unable to produce its like despite the challenge, concludes: thus was its truthfulness known, that it comes from God. The clarity of the tongue (mubīn) removes every pretext: the proof is offered without veil.

mots.racine: ل س ن / ع ر ب

versets.trad: And indeed it is in the scriptures of the ancients.
versets.commentaire: The Qurʾān accords with the earlier Books. Qushayrī: all this Book contains — accounts, tidings, and what belongs to God of the majesty due to Him — is conformable to what was revealed in the Scriptures sent before it. The unity of the message across the Books attests its single source.

versets.ar: أَوَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُمْ ءَايَةً أَن يَعْلَمَهُۥ عُلَمَٰٓؤُا۟ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ
versets.tr: awa-lam yakun lahum āyatan an yaʿlamahu ʿulamāʾu banī isrāʾīla
versets.trad: Is it not a sign for them that the scholars of the Children of Israel recognize it?
versets.commentaire: The recognition of the Qurʾān by the scholars of Israel is a sign. Qushayrī, in the flow of the accord of Scriptures, sees in this testimony an added proof: those who know the earlier Books find in it the foretelling. But the closed heart refuses even the witness who confounds it.

versets.trad: Had We sent it down upon some non-Arab
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī explains the sense: had He sent down this Book in a tongue other than theirs, they would not have understood it and would have said: ‘If it were in our tongue, we would have recognized it and believed in it.’ God thus removed their excuse and sealed the proof against them. The choice of clear Arabic leaves them no pretext.

versets.ar: فَقَرَأَهُۥ عَلَيْهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: fa-qaraʾahu ʿalayhim mā kānū bihi muʾminīna
versets.trad: and he had recited it to them, they would not have believed in it.
versets.commentaire: Even in a foreign tongue, they would not have believed. Qushayrī shows that unbelief lies not in the language but in the heart: God removed their pretext and sealed the proof against them. It is not the veil of language that stops them, it is the veil of their refusal.

versets.trad: Thus have We made it pass into the hearts of the criminals,
versets.commentaire: God makes the Qurʾān pass into the very hearts of the criminals. Qushayrī recalls His truthful knowledge of them and His prior decree of abandonment: the Word reaches them, but they will not believe until they see the chastisement, when neither faith nor regret avails. The Word passes through them without opening them.

mots.racine: ق ل ب / ج ر م

versets.ar: لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ
versets.tr: lā yuʾminūna bihi ḥattā yarawu l-ʿadhāba l-alīma
versets.trad: they will not believe in it until they see the painful chastisement,
versets.commentaire: They will not believe until they see the chastisement. Qushayrī: they will not believe before they see the torment on the Day of resurrection, when neither faith nor repentance will avail them. Faith that waits for the evidence of fire is no longer the faith that saves: it comes at the hour when the door closes.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / أ ل م

versets.trad: so that it comes upon them suddenly, while they do not perceive.
versets.commentaire: The chastisement comes suddenly, without their foreboding it. Qushayrī stresses the suddenness (baghta): the heedless one is seized at the very heart of his quiet. Whoever ceaselessly puts faith off until tomorrow is surprised by the hour when there is no more tomorrow.

versets.trad: Then they will say: “Are we to be granted respite?”
versets.commentaire: Caught off guard, they beg for a reprieve. Qushayrī, who recalls that God granted a thousand respites in this world, shows the vanity of this late plea: the respite was life itself, which they squandered. To beg for time when time is up is the confession of heedlessness.

versets.trad: Do they then seek to hasten Our chastisement?
versets.commentaire: They mocked by demanding the chastisement; now it comes. Qushayrī sets this mocking haste against the respite God long granted them: whoever presses the torment in defiance mismeasures the patience of the Merciful, who defers to leave a chance for return.

versets.trad: Have you considered: if We let them enjoy years of pleasure,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī comments: if We prolong their term and leave them long in their heedlessness, what will it avail them when the chastisement seizes them suddenly? The years of enjoyment are but a reprieve; the duration of pleasure does not redeem the instant of reckoning.

versets.trad: then there comes upon them what they were promised,
versets.commentaire: At last comes what they were promised. Qushayrī continues his questioning: what good is the long reprieve if the end is chastisement? The deferred promise is fulfilled, and the respite proves to have been but a delay before the inevitable.

versets.ar: مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُمَتَّعُونَ
versets.tr: mā aghnā ʿanhum mā kānū yumattaʿūna
versets.trad: of what avail to them will be that which they enjoyed?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī answers his own question: the enjoyment they relished availed them nothing when the chastisement seized them unawares. All the heedless one amasses vanishes at the hour of reckoning; there remains only what the heart has brought of rectitude.

versets.ar: وَمَآ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا لَهَا مُنذِرُونَ
versets.tr: wa-mā ahlaknā min qaryatin illā lahā mundhirūna
versets.trad: We have not destroyed any town but that it had warners,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God has annihilated the people of no town except after sending them the warner and showing them the proofs; and when they persisted in their denial, He chastised them. No ruin without prior warning: divine justice always precedes the chastisement.

versets.trad: by way of reminder; and We have never been unjust.
versets.commentaire: The reminder precedes the ruin: God is never unjust. Qushayrī insists: chastisement comes only after warning and the manifestation of proofs. “We have not been unjust”: the responsibility for the loss falls on whoever refused the reminder offered.

versets.trad: It was not the devils who brought it down: this is neither
versets.commentaire: It was not the devils who descended with the Qurʾān. Qushayrī opposes the descent of the trustworthy Spirit upon the Prophet's pure heart to the suggestions the devils breathe to their minions. The holy Word cannot pass through impure bearers: its pure source demands a pure channel.

versets.trad: fitting for them nor within their power,
versets.commentaire: The devils have neither the disposition nor the power for it. Qushayrī establishes the abyss between the Word of God and the whisper of the devils: what descends from the Real utterly surpasses their reach. Their very impotence attests the holiness of the Revelation.

versets.trad: and they are barred from hearing it.
versets.commentaire: The devils are kept far from the celestial hearing. Qushayrī widens it to a human sense: some possess hearing — perception — but are deprived of understanding, and do not answer the call; thus they merit from God the evil end. To hear without understanding is another form of exclusion.

versets.ar: فَلَا تَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُعَذَّبِينَ
versets.tr: fa-lā tadʿu maʿa llāhi ilāhan ākhara fa-takūna mina l-muʿadhdhabīna
versets.trad: So call not upon any other god besides God, lest you be among the chastised.
versets.commentaire: The call to absolute tawḥīd addressed to the Prophet and, through him, to all. Qushayrī, in his reading of the Names and Oneness, sees in this verse the summit of the way: to invoke none but the One. To associate anything with God is to enter the ranks of the chastised.

mots.racine: أ ل ه / أ خ ر

versets.trad: And warn your nearest kin.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes this command a lesson on the vanity of kinship: this teaches him that their kinship will not profit them, and that his intercession will not be accepted for them if they do not believe. The matter does not rest on lineage: Noah's unbelieving son gained nothing from his sonship; Abraham's unbelieving father gained nothing from his fatherhood. Blood does not save without faith.

mots.racine: ع ش ر / ق ر ب

versets.ar: وَٱخْفِضْ جَنَاحَكَ لِمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ
versets.tr: wa-khfiḍ janāḥaka li-mani ttabaʿaka mina l-muʾminīna
versets.trad: And lower your wing to the believers who follow you.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds this verse into a veritable code of companionship (ṣuḥba): soften your side, draw them near in fellowship, trail the hem of forbearance over their shortcomings, bear with their bad states, keep company with them with beautiful character, take on their burdens, have mercy on them all: if they fall ill, visit them; if they deprive you, give to them; if they wrong you, pardon them; if they fall short in My right, excuse them, intercede and seek forgiveness for them.

mots.racine: خ ف ض / ج ن ح

versets.ar: فَإِنْ عَصَوْكَ فَقُلْ إِنِّى بَرِيٓءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ
versets.tr: fa-in ʿaṣawka fa-qul innī barīʾun mimmā taʿmalūna
versets.trad: If they disobey you, say: “I am quit of what you do.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī clarifies: do not act as they do; their whole reckoning is Ours, save where We have commanded you to apply a limit (ḥadd) — then let no tenderness divert you from carrying out Our prescription. Gentleness toward the believers does not abolish the duty to declare oneself innocent of the evil committed.

versets.trad: And put your trust in the Mighty, the Merciful,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī makes this verse an invitation to total surrender: take refuge in Us, cling to Us, take Us as intercessor with Us, be perpetually through Us: if you speak, speak through Us; if you act, act through Us. And: “Put your trust in the Mighty”: you will find might in both abodes, for mighty is whoever relies on the Mighty.

mots.racine: ع ز ز / ر ح م

versets.trad: who sees you when you stand,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: by this verse He cuts him off from the sight of creatures, for whoever knows he is under the gaze of the Real watches over the least of his states and the secrets of his affairs with Him. To know one is seen by God transforms the night vigil into intimate presence, where no other gaze counts.

versets.trad: and your movements among those who prostrate.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī lightens the toil of worship through awareness of the divine gaze: no fatigue for one who knows he is under his Master's gaze; to bear the anchored mountains on the edge of an eyelid would be light for whoever beholds his Lord. And by allusion: “your movement among those who prostrate” — among your companions: they are stars and you among them the full moon, or moons and you the sun.

versets.trad: Truly He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī modulates the two Names with tenderness: the All-Hearing (al-Samīʿ) of the lovers' moaning, the All-Knowing (al-ʿAlīm) of the knowers' yearning; the All-Hearing of the sinners' plaint, the All-Knowing of the obedient ones' states. God hears the breath of love as the sigh of repentance.

versets.ar: هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن تَنَزَّلُ ٱلشَّيَٰطِينُ
versets.tr: hal unabbiʾukum ʿalā man tanazzalu sh-shayāṭīnu
versets.trad: Shall I tell you upon whom the devils descend?
versets.commentaire: Opposite the trustworthy Spirit who descends upon the pure heart, here is upon whom the devils descend. Qushayrī opposes the two descents: the holy Word upon the Prophet's heart, and diabolic suggestions upon the deniers and soothsayers. The nature of the receptacle reveals the nature of what descends.

versets.trad: They descend upon every sinful liar.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: He shows that the devils descend upon the unbelievers and soothsayers, breathing into them their lying suggestions. The devil's receptacle is the great liar (affāk) laden with sin: the heart's impurity attracts the impure inspiration, as purity attracts the trustworthy Spirit.

versets.trad: to whom they pass on hearsay, but most of them are liars.
versets.commentaire: They lend an ear and pass on, but most lie. Qushayrī opposes this deceitful hearsay to the sure transmission of the trustworthy Spirit who “heard from the Lord, retained and conveyed.” The soothsayer snatches scraps and lies; the prophet receives the Word intact and truthful.

mots.racine: ل ق ي / س م ع

versets.trad: As for the poets, the erring follow them.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī links the mention of the poets to that of the devils: after evoking the Revelation and what the angels bring from God, then what the devils breathe to their minions, He joined to them the poets who wander in falsehood, who assail people's honor, who depart from rectitude in their comparisons, who promise what they do not keep and follow the path of lying. The erring (al-ghāwūn) follow those who wander astray.

mots.racine: ت ب ع / غ و ي

versets.ar: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى كُلِّ وَادٍ يَهِيمُونَ
versets.tr: a-lam tara annahum fī kulli wādin yahīmūna
versets.trad: Do you not see that they wander in every valley,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī paints them wandering “in every valley”: they roam in falsehood and depart from the measure of rectitude. The erring poet follows his whim from theme to theme, without bearing or truth. Wandering (hayamān) without direction is the image of the soul that has no Qibla.

versets.trad: and that they say what they do not do?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sums up the erring poet's vice: they promise of themselves what they do not keep and take the path of lying. The divorce between word and deed is the mark of wandering: to say the beautiful without living it is to betray the word itself.

versets.ar: إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّٰلِحَٰتِ وَذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا وَٱنتَصَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ظُلِمُوا۟ وَسَيَعْلَمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَىَّ مُنقَلَبٍ يَنقَلِبُونَ
versets.tr: illā lladhīna āmanū wa-ʿamilū ṣ-ṣāliḥāti wa-dhakarū llāha kathīran wa-ntaṣarū min baʿdi mā ẓulimū wa-sa-yaʿlamu lladhīna ẓalamū ayya munqalabin yanqalibūna
versets.trad: Save those who believe, do righteous deeds, remember God much, and defend themselves after they have been wronged. And those who do wrong will soon know to what a place of return they will return!
versets.commentaire: The exception saves the believing poets: their poetry is then free of those blameworthy flaws, says Qushayrī, citing: “Poetry is the speech of man: its good is like his good, and its ugliness like his ugliness.” Faith, righteous deeds, abundant remembrance of God and lawful defense redeem it. And the seal: “Those who have done wrong will soon know to what a place of return they will return” — they will know the evil they did, repent of what they sent ahead, and at last believe what they had denied.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / ك ث ر

mots.def: intaṣara: to defend oneself (after wrong suffered).
